questions.question: You compute the cosine similarity between two text chunk vectors and get a score of 0.92. A second pair of chunks returns a score of 0.07. Which statement BEST describes what these scores tell you?
options: The first pair are nearly identical in meaning; the second pair are semantically unrelated.
The first pair are long documents; the second pair are short snippets.
The first pair were embedded with the same model; the second pair used different models.
The first pair share many exact keywords; the second pair share none.
questions.explanation: Cosine similarity measures the angle between two vectors in semantic space, ranging from -1 (opposite direction) through 0 (perpendicular / unrelated) to 1 (same direction / same meaning). A score of 0.92 means the two vectors point nearly in the same direction — the texts carry very similar meaning. A score of 0.07 means the vectors are nearly perpendicular — the texts are semantically unrelated. The metric deliberately ignores vector length, so document length (option B) does not affect it. Whether the same model was used (option C) is a validity concern but not what the score encodes. Keyword overlap (option D) is what TF-IDF measures, not cosine similarity on neural embeddings, which capture meaning even across different wordings.

questions.question: Many production RAG systems report that cosine similarity and dot-product search give identical rankings for their embedding vectors. What is the correct technical reason for this?
options: Cosine similarity and dot product are always mathematically identical for any pair of vectors.
Most modern embedding models return L2-normalized vectors (length = 1), which makes cosine similarity reduce to the dot product.
Vector databases automatically convert dot product scores to cosine scores before returning results.
Both metrics compute the Euclidean distance between vectors, so they must agree.
questions.explanation: The cosine similarity formula is (A · B) / (||A|| × ||B||). When every vector is L2-normalized its magnitude ||A|| = ||B|| = 1, so the denominator becomes 1 and the formula reduces to the plain dot product A · B. Models such as OpenAI text-embedding-3 return normalized vectors by default, which is why the two metrics are interchangeable in those systems — but only for normalized vectors (option A is wrong in the general case). Vector databases do not silently convert metrics (option C), and Euclidean distance is a third, distinct metric, not the same as either cosine or dot product (option D).

questions.question: A team has already indexed 2 million document chunks in a vector database using OpenAI text-embedding-3-small. They now want to switch to bge-m3 for better multilingual coverage. What must they do?
options: Update only the query-time embedding call; the stored chunk vectors are compatible with any model that outputs 1,024-dimensional vectors.
Re-embed all 2 million chunks with bge-m3 and replace every vector in the database, because embeddings are model-specific.
Run a one-time alignment matrix to translate text-embedding-3-small vectors into bge-m3 space without re-embedding.
Keep the existing vectors and add bge-m3 vectors only for newly ingested documents going forward.
questions.explanation: Embeddings are model-specific: a vector produced by text-embedding-3-small and a vector produced by bge-m3 live in completely different semantic spaces, even if they happen to share the same number of dimensions. Comparing a query vector from model A against chunk vectors from model B produces meaningless similarity scores. There is no universal alignment matrix that reliably converts between arbitrary embedding spaces (option C). A hybrid approach (option D) would make the index inconsistent: queries embedded with bge-m3 could not meaningfully compare against chunks embedded with the old model. The only correct path is to re-embed the entire corpus with the new model and rebuild the index.

questions.question: A startup is building a RAG-based customer-support assistant for a Korean e-commerce platform. Their documents are Korean product descriptions mixed with English brand names and technical SKUs. They have no GPU infrastructure and a tight per-query cost budget. Which embedding model choice is MOST appropriate as a starting point?
options: A Korean-specialized model such as ko-sroberta, because the documents are primarily Korean.
OpenAI text-embedding-3-small via API, because it handles Korean acceptably, requires no GPU, and has the lowest friction for a first MVP.
all-MiniLM-L6-v2 from SBERT, because its 384-dimensional vectors minimize storage costs.
bge-large-en, because it ranks highest on the English MTEB leaderboard.
questions.explanation: The scenario describes a first MVP with Korean + English mixed content, no GPU, and a tight budget. OpenAI text-embedding-3-small handles Korean acceptably out of the box, requires only an API call (no infrastructure), and is the lowest-friction path — exactly matching the rule of thumb stated in the lecture. Korean-specialized models (option A) may struggle with the English brand names and technical terms mixed into the documents. all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (option C) is English-focused; its multilingual performance would be unreliable for Korean text. bge-large-en (option D) is an English-only model and would perform poorly on Korean regardless of its English benchmark ranking. Once a working baseline is established, the team should A/B test bge-m3 and Korean-specialized models against their own evaluation set before switching.

questions.question: A healthcare company must build a RAG system over confidential patient records written entirely in Korean. Regulations prohibit sending any patient data to external APIs. Which embedding path should they choose, and why?
options: OpenAI text-embedding-3-large, because it produces the highest-quality vectors and handles Korean well.
Cohere embed-multilingual-v3.0 via API, because it is the strongest multilingual commercial option.
A self-hosted Korean-specialized model (e.g., ko-sroberta) or bge-m3, because the data cannot leave their network and the corpus is purely Korean.
all-MiniLM-L6-v2 self-hosted, because it is the smallest and easiest model to deploy on CPU.
questions.explanation: Data-privacy constraints make self-hosting mandatory — options A and B both require sending data to a third-party API, which is ruled out by regulation. Given that the corpus is 100 percent Korean, a Korean-specialized model (ko-sroberta, ko-sbert) is the strongest candidate because it was trained on Korean corpora and uses a Korean-aware tokenizer, which means better morphological handling and accurate token counts. bge-m3 self-hosted is also a valid choice because it supports Korean among 100+ languages and can run on private infrastructure; it is especially useful if any English-language medical literature might be added later. all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (option D) is primarily an English model; self-hosting it would satisfy the privacy requirement but would likely deliver poor Korean retrieval quality — privacy compliance alone does not make it a good fit for this use case.
